at least one bar in America was always tuned into the shows I helped produc=
e. But this past Sunday offered me a fresh perspective on the wider impact =
of industry disputes.
Right before the much-anticipated LSU-USC game was about to start, my wife=
=E2=80=99s customers found themselves staring at blank screens, victims of =
a broadcast dispute they had no awareness of.

=E2=80=9CDirecTV has long been the backbone for sports bars across the U.S.=
, offering packages that other providers just can=E2=80=99t match
for commercial businesses. I know the complexities involved. However, seein=
g how this situation affected my wife=E2=80=99s pub=E2=80=94a small busines=
s that fought to stay afloat through the pandemic=E2=80=94hit close to home=
=2E The disappointment in the room wa=
s palpable, and I realized her pub=E2=80=
=99s experience wasn=E2=80=99t unique. Thousands of bars across the country=
 likely faced the same frustration, as loyal patrons had to scramble for ot=
her options to catch a game that should have brought people together. It=E2=
=80=99s moments like this that underscore just how far-reaching these dispu=
tes can be, even down to local businesses that have been part of the fabric=
 of their communities for generations.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A producer

 offended by you calling ESPN a =E2=80=98network=E2=80=99? Doesn=E2=80=99t =

aro planning on a rebrand?=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A Varsity subscriber

On Pitaro=E2=80=99s
high-school days: =E2=80=9CI loved listening to your podcast with Jimmy. I =
never shared with you the photos in our high-school yearbook that showed Ji=
mmy as =E2=80=98best looking,=E2=80=99 =E2=80=98best physique,=E2=80=99 and=

school. If so, tell him I said hello.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94Pitaro=E2=80=99s hi=
